articles.content:
A Star Suddenly Brightened, Then Faded Into Darkness. Astronomers Say It May Have Turned Into a Black Hole
If confirmed, this disappearing act might provide the closest and best observational evidence for the birth of a black hole
Over roughly the past decade, a gleaming star some 2.5 million light-years from Earth in the Andromeda galaxy suddenly brightened, then drastically dimmed. Now, some astronomers say the vanishing act may have been a dying star turning into a black hole, according to a study published February 12 in the journal Science. If confirmed, the event provides the strongest observational evidence yet of this type of stellar transformation.
“It used to be one of the brightest stars in the Andromeda galaxy,” says study co-author Kishalay De, an astronomer at Columbia University, to Nell Greenfieldboyce at NPR. “Today, it is nowhere to be seen, even with the most sensitive telescopes.”
De and his colleagues happened upon the fading star while combing through data captured by NASA’s NEOWISE space telescope, which observed the sky from 2009 to 2024. They were looking for celestial objects that changed brightness in infrared wavelengths of light when they spotted a yellow supergiant star called M31-2014-DS1, which ramped up its infrared glow starting in 2014. But data from additional telescopes helped reveal that from 2017 to 2022, the star’s visible light faded to at least a ten-thousandth of its original brightness, becoming basically undetectable.
“I got goosebumps when I saw it disappearing into darkness,” De tells Lee Billings at Scientific American. The star has grown so dim that the James Webb Space Telescope can barely see its infrared light, he tells NPR.
Did you know? The galaxy next door
The Andromeda galaxy is considered the closest major galaxy to the one we call home, the Milky Way. It’s shaped like a spiral, just like ours, and it helps astronomers better understand the Milky Way’s structure and evolution. (It’s hard to study the galaxy that we’re embedded within.)
Leading theories of stellar death suggest that when a star about eight or more times as massive as the sun runs out of nuclear fuel in its core, it collapses under its own weight. That creates a dense neutron star that can blast away the star’s outer shells in a fiery supernova explosion.
Sometimes, however, an extremely massive star will skip the theatrics, according to theoretical calculations. It will continue to quietly implode until it forms a black hole. M31-2014-DS1 may undergone such a silent transformation, the researchers suggest. Computer simulations hint that the star was initially about 13 times the mass of the sun, and after shedding its outer layers, was only around 5 solar masses and shrouded by dust soon before its death.
Failed supernovas are hard to find and observe. Scientists have spotted only one other candidate, supergiant NGC 6946-BH1, which was reported in a 2015 study. Located about 19 million light-years from Earth, it’s much farther than the recently identified fading star and thus harder to measure. But the new candidate’s characteristics resemble those of the older one.
Not everyone is convinced by the new study’s conclusions, however. Emma Beasor, an astronomer at Liverpool John Moores University in England who was not involved in the work, tells Science’s Daniel Clery that while the research “changes the entire picture of stellar evolution,” the study authors could have witnessed something other than a failed supernova. Last month, she and her colleagues published a study in the Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society suggesting that both M31-2014-DS1 and NGC 6946-BH1 might instead represent stars that collided and merged, and are now covered in dust.
If M31-2014-DS1 actually became a black hole, it should stay dark and dead—something Beasor will be keeping a close eye on, per Science. “Unfortunately, it is a waiting game.”
Christopher Kochanek, an astronomer at Ohio State University who co-authored study on the past black hole birth candidate but was not involved in the new work, agrees with the new study’s findings and does not find Beasor’s alternative suggestion convincing, reports Scientific American. Still, he will also be eagerly awaiting future observations of—or lack thereof—M31-2014-DS1.
“Fundamentally, the only way to clearly answer this either way is that one thing distinguishes the black hole case from any other scenario,” Kochanek tells NPR. “And that is that death is forever. Ultimately, it needs to fade to black.”

articles.title: Berlin Wall Section in Trelleborg in Trelleborg, Sweden
articles.author: Redação
articles.excerpt: In Sweden’s southernmost port city, Trelleborg, a section of the once-infamous Berlin Wall stands quietly outside the city museum. Measuring about 13 feet by 3 feet (4 by 1 meters), the slab is protected by plexiglass that preserves its original graffiti, a bold message reading “SAVE OUR EARTH NOW.”

articles.title: How Often Do You Fart? This 'Smart Underwear' Can Keep Track, Because Figuring Out a Baseline Is Important for Science
articles.author: Redação
articles.excerpt:
How Often Do You Fart? This ‘Smart Underwear’ Can Keep Track, Because Figuring Out a Baseline Is Important for Science
Researchers have launched a study to find a typical range for flatulence, which...
articles.content:
How Often Do You Fart? This ‘Smart Underwear’ Can Keep Track, Because Figuring Out a Baseline Is Important for Science
Researchers have launched a study to find a typical range for flatulence, which has been harder to measure than you might expect
How many times do you fart a day? It sounds like the sort of question a little brother and his friends might ask while barely holding back laughter. Studying flatulence has medical value, however, and measuring how frequently someone passes gas is harder than you might expect.
“Up until now, studying flatulence meant either asking people to count their own farts—which doesn’t work well—or bringing them into a clinical setting and using extremely invasive techniques like rectal tubes for short, controlled measurements,” Brantley Hall, a biologist at the University of Maryland, tells Gizmodo’s Gayoung Lee.
Hall and his colleagues have recently created the first wearable continuous fart-measuring device. This “smart underwear” is a coin-size gadget that snaps into any pair of undies right around where you’d imagine a fart-measuring tool should go.
Now, the researchers want adults all over the country—and fart spectrum—to try it out. Hall has launched the Human Flatus Atlas, a research study recruiting smart underwear users to learn about people’s flatulence patterns and align them with what they eat and the makeup of their microbiomes.
No accurate fart baseline currently exists, so it’s hard for doctors to know when a patient’s numbers are off, which could be caused by an underlying health problem. Estimates often cite that people tend to pass gas about 14 times per day.
But a recent test with the smart underwear, published in the December 2025 issue of the journal Biosensors and Bioelectronics: X, found that 19 healthy participants farted an average of 32 times per day. Still, the daily flatulence widely ranged, with a minimum of 4 farts in one day and a maximum of 59. Participants had worn the flatulence-measuring devices for about 11 hours per day, on average, for a week.
Fun fact: Fish might fart to communicate
Some herring seem to expel air from their rear ends to talk to one another. The noises are aptly called fast repetitive ticks, or FRTs.
Most people’s farts are mainly made of hydrogen, carbon dioxide and nitrogen. The smart underwear measures hydrogen, specifically, because it’s produced only by the wearer’s gut microbes.
“It tells you how active your microbes are, how much of the food you eat is being metabolized by your gut microbes,” Hall tells WTOP News’ John Domen. “So, someone who farts more is either eating a lot of high fiber food, which is reaching the microbes, which turn into flatus, or maybe they have a condition like small intestine bacterial overgrowth. … So, it tells us a lot about how your digestion is working.”
In the published study, the smart underwear was able to register additional hydrogen after people ingested prebiotic fiber, correctly identifying a diet-driven shift in gut microbe activity 94.7 percent of the time. “Objective measurement gives us an opportunity to increase scientific rigor in an area that’s been difficult to study,” Hall says in a statement. “Think of it like a continuous glucose monitor, but for intestinal gas.”
For the Human Flatus Atlas project, the researchers are seeking individuals from various groups established in previous work: zen digesters who fart very little despite eating lots of fiber, hydrogen hyperproducers who experience frequent flatulence and normal farters who are in between.
The team will gather stool samples from members of the first two categories to analyze what gas-producing microbes are up to at both ends of the spectrum. The project should help determine what a standard fart quantity range is for adults in the United States. Hall tells the Baltimore Banner’s Darreonna Davis that about 900 potential candidates have already shown interest, and that his lab members have started to send out some smart underwear devices.
For three consecutive days, participants will wear the gadgets for 12 hours per day and upload photos of their meals to the Human Flatus Atlas app. Then, they’ll fill out a questionnaire about the experience.
“There’s many potential causes of [gastrointestinal] distress, and there’s many tests for the different ones. This adds another test that we can help figure out if this is a true problem or this is not a problem, and help them get the right medical care,” Hall tells the Baltimore Banner. “That’s my ultimate goal.”

articles.title: 16 Oscar Winners Who Starred on Soap Operas
articles.author: Sabrina Reed
articles.excerpt: If Michael B. Jordan wins the Oscar for Best Actor at the 2026 Academy Awards, heâll be joining an exclusive club thatâs gone overlooked by film lovers who pay little attention to broadcast staples....
articles.content:
If Michael B. Jordan wins the Oscar for Best Actor at the 2026 Academy Awards, heâll be joining an exclusive club thatâs gone overlooked by film lovers who pay little attention to broadcast staples. Heâll become a soap star whoâs gone on to win an Oscar.
The genre, which is often looked down upon despite being the medium of some of the most talented TV stars in the business, is credited as being a training ground for the industry. Soap operas are notorious for their long hours of filming and the amount of material that must be shot and performed to meet the goal of putting on a show every weekday.
This rigorous television format has churned out quite a few household names. Itâs also been the home of Hollywood movie stars looking to TV for their next big role. Curious to see which Oscar-winning actors were a part of the soap world? You may be surprised by who made this list!
- Anne Baxter
- Celeste Holm
- Jane Wyman
- Kim Hunter
- Donna Reed
- Dorothy Malone
- Elizabeth Taylor
- Beatrice Straight
- George Kennedy
- Christopher Walken
- Marisa Tomei
- Kevin Kline
- Tommy Lee Jones
- Melissa Leo
- Julianne Moore
- Allison Janney
Anne Baxter
In 1947, Anne Baxter won Best Supporting Actress for her performance as Sophie MacDonald in The Razorâs Edge. The film, centered on a World War I vet searching for his purpose in life, saw Baxter step into the role of a woman whose life was marked by tragedy. She had to play an alcoholic haunted by the death of her husband and baby. Though Sophie wanted a better life, she ultimately succumbed to addiction and died in the movie. Baxter was praised for the nuance of her portrayal.
By the â80s, the actress had turned her attention to television. She starred as Victoria Cabot on ABCâs Hotel. The soap opera was set in the St. Gregory Hotel in San Francisco, where the posh Victoria ran the establishment with the help of Peter McDermott, the general manager. The series was known for its rotating cast of guests.Â
Hotel briefly reunited Baxter with Bette Davis, who played Victoriaâs sister-in-law, Laura Trent, in the pilot. The co-stars were rivals in the 1951 Best Picture Winner, All About Eve, and were nominated for Best Actress. The soap opera ran from 1983 to 1987. Baxter was written out of the series upon her death in 1985. Victoria left half of the St. Gregory to Peter in her will.
Celeste Holm
Before her turn on ABCâs Loving as Isabella Alden, Celeste Holm was an accomplished Broadway and film star. She won Best Supporting Actress in 1948 for playing Anne Dettrey in A Gentlemanâs Agreement.
The movie, which follows a journalist who pretends to be Jewish to expose antisemitism in New York City and in affluent communities in Connecticut, saw her portray Smithâs weekly fashion editor Anne Dettrey. Holmâs character is charming, friendly, and very clear about her views as a woman who opposes antisemitism and the mistreatment of others simply because of their differences.
As her career progressed, Holm spent more time on TV than in film or on stage. She made her soap debut in 1981 as Lauren Roberts on CBSâs As the World Turns. Then, in 1985, Holm was cast as Anna Rossini in Falcon Crest for a six-episode arc. She played the widowed mother of Cassandra Wilder and Damon Ross, who was institutionalized after setting fire to the Falcon Crest mansion in an attempt to kill Angela Channing. Anna believed Angela was the reason her husband committed suicide.
Holm made her first appearance on Loving in 1986 as Lydia Woodhouse. She received a Daytime Emmy nomination for her guest role. The actress would later return to step into the shoes of Isabelle Aldean in 1991, becoming the third actress to portray the wealthy familyâs matriarch. She left the soap in 1992.
Jane Wyman
Fans of Falcon Crest know Jane Wyman as the scheming winemaker Angela Channing, but before she was the soapâs matriarch to a scandalous family, she was an in-demand film talent.Â
Wyman won a Golden Globe and the Oscar for Best Supporting Actress in 1949 due to her portrayal of Belinda MacDonald in Johnny Belinda. She played a deaf-mute girl whose rape results in a pregnancy. When Belinda befriends a doctor, her life improves.Â
It would be nearly 40 years before Wyman would win a Golden Globe again, this time for Falcon Crest in 1984. The actress starred as Angela for the primetime soap operaâs entire run, from 1981 to 1990.
Kim Hunter
One of the most iconic lines in the history of cinema is Marlon Brandoâs yell of âStellaâ in A Streetcar Named Desire, and itâs Kim Hunter who played Stella Kowalski. She won Best Supporting Actress in 1952 for her performance as Stanleyâs pregnant wife and Blanche Duboisâ sister, who is caught in the middle of her husbandâs violent spiral that results in Blancheâs rape and mental breakdown ahead of the babyâs birth.
Though Hunter continued to star in movies throughout her career, she developed a name for herself in the realm of television through guest appearances. She landed a longer arc in 1979 as Nola Madison on ABCâs The Edge of Night. Hunter played an actress with a drinking problem whose career was in its twilight years. She was initially supposed to be on the soap for only a few months, but her role was extended when the writers came up with the idea of casting Nola in a film.
When speaking about her time on The Edge of Night, Hunter told the Television Academy that âit was exhausting. It really was. And I know bloody well that Iâd never want to do it again, but Iâm not sorry that I did do it because it was a very, very interesting experience.â The actress was referring to the fast pace of soap operas, which saw her taping five episodes in one week. Hunter was nominated for a Daytime Emmy in 1980 for her work on the series.
Donna Reed
Dallas was the last credit Oscar winner Donna Reed would add to her filmography. She won Best Supporting Actress in 1954 for her role as Alma âLoreneâ Burke in From Here to Eternity. The film, which won Best Picture and was famous for its steamy beach-kiss scene, starred Reed as a social club worker who loses her love on the day Pearl Harbor was attacked.
Aside from her own sitcom, The Donna Reed Show, and a few guest appearances on various TV series, Reed was mostly known for her work in film. But she did enter the world of soaps to play Miss Ellie Ewing Farlow when the characterâs originator, Barbara Bel Geddes, was temporarily replaced due to health issues.
Reed portrayed Ellieâthe Ewing matriarch who loved fiercely, cared deeply for her familyâs ranch, and believed in the strength that can be found in the family unitâfrom 1984 to 1985 until Geddes returned.
Dorothy Malone
The 1957 winner of Best Supporting Actress, Dorothy Malone, is best known for playing Constance MacKenzie in the ABC soap opera Peyton Place, which premiered in 1964. This was just seven years after Malone gave her award-winning performance in Written on the Wind.
In the movie, Malone plays Marylee Hadley, the self-destructive daughter of an oil baron. The film is a melodrama that saw her character accusing the lead, Mitch Wayne, of murder in a fit of jealousy and grief over the death of her brother. It had the kind of plot that wouldnât be out of place in a soap opera.
Peyton Place saw Malone step into the role of a single mother determined to protect her daughter, even if it meant being overbearing. She was twice nominated for a Golden Globe and served as the series' lead until 1968. Malone was written out for complaining that Constance was being slighted in storylines. In response, the actress sued the showâs production company, 20th Century Fox, for $1.6 million. The case was settled.
Malone later reprised her role of Constance in 1977 for the made-for-TV movie, Murder in Peyton Place, and again in 1985 for the telefilm Peyton Place: The Next Generation.
Elizabeth Taylor
Screen legend Elizabeth Taylor is a two-time Oscar winner whose soap legacy is brief but memorable. She took home the Academyâs top acting honor in 1961 for Butterfield 8 and Whoâs Afraid of Virginia Woolf? in 1967.
By the early â80s, after her film career wound down, Taylor turned her attention to television, where she made guest appearances and starred in TV movies. Her most significant role, besides the 1985 miniseries North and South, was on ABCâs General Hospital in 1981. The actress originated the character Helena Cassadine, a villainess who cursed Luke and Laura on the day of the wedding.
Taylor was a big fan of General Hospital and was happy to be a part of the soap opera. The late Anthony Geary (who played Luke) once described how the star felt about the daytime drama, stating, âLuke and Laura's love story made Elizabeth feel very nostalgic. She said it reminded her of her youth and the great romance films of the old studio system.â
Though Taylor was only in a three-episode arc, she left a lasting impression on the series. The only other time sheâd appear in a soap opera was in a 1984 episode of Hotel.
Beatrice Straight
Life imitated art with Beatrice Straightâs soap legacy. She won Best Supporting Actress in 1977 for her portrayal of Louise Schumacher in the satirical dramedy Network, about a struggling TV program. Straight went on to star in the short-lived primetime soap opera Kingâs Crossing in 1982, which only lasted seven episodes before it was pulled from ABCâs line-up. She played Louisa Beauchamp, Nan's distant aunt, who lives on a sinister ranch in an equally sinister house described as looking like the Bates mansion in Psycho.
George Kennedy
One of the few Oscar-winning men to play a significant role in a soap opera was George Kennedy. The World War II vet began acting after he served 16 years in the Army. Kennedy was prolific, starring in over 200 films throughout his career. He won Best Supporting Actor in 1968 for his performance as Clarence "Dragline" Slidell, the chain gang leader who goes from Lukeâs nemesis to his friend, in the prison drama Cool Hand Luke.
Kennedyâs most prominent television role was on Dallas as Carter McKay, a rancher and business rival of J.R. Ewing, who arrived in the show's last three seasons. He was on the daytime drama from 1988 to its final season in 1991. He reprised his role for the TV movies Dallas: J.R. Returns (1996) and Dallas: War of the Ewings (1998).
The actorâs last TV role, though not his final acting credit, was on the CBS soap Young and the Restless. He played Albert Miller, Victor Newmanâs father, whoâd abandoned his family when Victor was a child. Kennedy joined the daytime drama for a brief guest arc in 2003 and later returned in 2010 for Victorâs A Christmas Carol-like episode, where Albert appears as a ghost.
Christopher Walken
Known for his unique voice as much as his memorable roles, Christopher Walken got his start in Hollywood as a child. One of his first roles was Mike Bauer, the sensitive son of Bill and Bert Bauer, whoâd grow up to become a respected attorney in his community on The Young and the Restless.
Walken shared the credit with his brother Glenn. They originated the role in 1954 and remained on the show until 1956. 23 years later, Walken won Best Supporting Actor for The Deer Hunter in 1979. He played Corporal Nikanor âNickâ Chevotarevich, an enlisted service member whose life derails due to PTSD from his experiences in the Vietnam War and ends tragically from suicide.
Marisa Tomei
As the World Turns was Marisa Tomeiâs first acting credit. She was a college student when she landed the role of Marcy Thompson, a teenager taken in by the McColl family whoâd fall for their son Kirk before eventually marrying a prince. Tomei was on the soap from 1983 to 1985.
â10 years after her first role, she won Best Supporting Actress in 1993 for her portrayal of Mona Lisa Vito in My Cousin Vinny. Tomei played the titular characterâs fiancÃ©e, who helps him win the case against his cousin. The scene in which Lisa explains why Bill Gambini and Stan Rothenstein couldnât have been the murderers, using her knowledge of cars, is one of the most iconic in film history.
Kevin Kline
Similar to a few actors on this list, Kevin Klineâs career began on a soap opera. His first acting credit was Woody Reed on the CBS series Search for Tomorrow. He joined the program in 1976, playing a âcad,â as PBS put it, until 1977.
Kline wouldnât enter the film scene until 1982 with Sophieâs Choice. Seven years later, in 1989, Â he won Best Supporting Actor for his turn as Otto West in A Fish Called Wanda. The heist comedy followed diamond thieves as they searched for their leaderâs hidden treasure. Otto was a philosophy student who was dreadful at robbery.
Tommy Lee Jones
Before Tommy Lee Jones won Best Supporting Actor in 1994 for his performance as U.S. Deputy Marshal Sam Gerard in The Fugitive, he starred on ABCâs One Life to Live. Dr. Mark Toland was his first TV credit, and he recurred as the character from 1971 to 1975. The good doctor was notably suave and loving before a character switch that saw him completely flip. The move came right after Jones had given notice that heâd be leaving the soap.
In 1994, the actor told Entertainment Weekly that his character became an âA-one nut boyâ and that over a weekend, he went from upstanding to Dr. Hyde. Jones said, âThey had me kill my parents! About the nicest thing I did was take drugs.â
âFor The Fugitive, Jones was on the right side of the law, playing the no-nonsense U.S. Marshal who comes to see that Dr. Richard Kimble has been framed for the murder of his wife. The role is part of a line of law enforcement portrayals for which Jones is known.
Melissa Leo
For Melissa Leo, playing Linda Warner on All My Children from 1984 to 1988 was like graduate school. The ABC soap opera was her first credit. She told Zap2it in 2011 that sheâd never turned her nose up to the genre. Leo said:
âI went into it knowing I would get a chance to act â maybe not every day, but a few times a week â and (there was) the learning a whole new thing every day, and being able to dance with television without knowing what was coming down the pike for your character. Then, you got to see what youâd done. If you didnât sit just around being critical of yourself, but you learnedâ¦â
âIn her time playing Cliff Warnerâs scheming little sister, Leo honed her skills as an actress. She was nominated for a Golden Globe in 1985 for her performance. But her first prestigious win would be in 2011 for The Fighter, a boxing biopic about brothers Micky Ward and Dick Eklund. Leo played their mother, Alice Eklund-Ward, who was Mickeyâs manager. She won Best Supporting Actress at the 83rd Academy Award ceremony.
Julianne Moore
The soap world is where Julianne Moore got her start. Her first credit was for The Edge of Night in 1984. She played Carmen Engler in a handful of episodes. Then, in 1985, she became a series regular on As the World Turns, playing Frannie Hughes.
Later, Moore would pull double duty, stepping into the role of Sabrina, Frannieâs sister whoâd been born to a different mother and who the family had thought died. The actress remained on the series until 1988, but returned in 2010 to bid the soap farewell as it came to an end.
âFive years after her final turn as Frannie, Moore won Best Actress for Still Alice in 2015. She starred as the titular character, Dr. Howland, a linguistics professor whoâs diagnosed with early-onset Alzheimerâs.
Allison Janney
While her most iconic role is C.J. Cregg on The West Wing, one of Allison Janneyâs first acting credits was as the housemaid Ginger on Guiding Light. She appeared on the soap on and off from 1993 to 1995. Her part was notably comedic with a terrible Brooklyn accent.
Janney is best known for her work on television, but she won Best Supporting Actress in 2018 for the ice skating biopic I, Tonya. She played Tonya Hardingâs abusive mother, LaVona Golden.

articles.title: Bird Watchers Flock to Montreal to Catch a Glimpse of Canada's First Known European Robin
articles.author: Redação
articles.excerpt:
Bird Watchers Flock to Montreal to Catch a Glimpse of Canada’s First Known European Robin
Experts don’t know how the little songbird traveled across the Atlantic Ocean
The new year seems to have...
articles.content:
Bird Watchers Flock to Montreal to Catch a Glimpse of Canada’s First Known European Robin
Experts don’t know how the little songbird traveled across the Atlantic Ocean
The new year seems to have brought a new visitor to Canada.
Bird watcher Sabrina Jacob met the guest, a European robin, in Montreal when she heard it singing while taking out the trash on January 7. She managed to take a quick video of it in a tree, which she posted to the Cornell Lab of Ornithology’s eBird tool. The sighting marks the first time the species has been seen in Canada. It has been spotted only a handful of times elsewhere in North America.
“I watch birds, so I was like, ‘I don’t know this sound.’ I just looked up, and I don’t know this bird either. He came straight up in front of me for two seconds, and I took the video,” Jacob tells CTV News’ Christine Long. She was shocked when a friend told her it was a European robin, per City News Everywhere’s David Zura and Joseph Ryan.
European robins have a characteristic orange face and chest and are usually found throughout western Eurasia and parts of North Africa. They are not close relatives of the American robin, despite their similar names.
Quick fact: Why do the two robins share similar names?
The American robin got its name because of its looks—it has an orange chest, like the European robin.
Since Jacob’s sighting, bird enthusiasts have traveled from all over North America hoping to catch a glimpse of Canada’s first known European robin. A man even told Global News’ Dan Spector that he’d flown from Victoria, British Columbia for the occasion and would be sleeping in his car.
“It’s wild for a lot of reasons,” Maggie MacPherson, an evolutionary ecologist at Trent University in Canada, tells the Guardian’s Danielle Beurteaux. European robins don’t always migrate, and those that do usually travel short distances, so it’s highly improbable that the bird intentionally crossed the Atlantic Ocean, she says. “That makes this sighting just amazing.”
How the now-famous bird arrived in Montreal is still up for debate, though two main hypotheses are that it was either swept across the ocean by a storm or hitched a ride on a ship.
“I doubt he was carried in some luggage in a plane and released here, and it’s possible he’s been here for a while. He could have been here for weeks or months, and maybe he did come during migration in the fall,” Joel Coutu, cited as a bird behaviorist by CTV News, tells the outlet. The European robin’s presence is so surprising that some people online wondered if artificial intelligence may have been involved in posted images, according to City News Everywhere.
This winter, Montreal has plunged to its coldest temperatures since 2018, but the little bird should be able to survive as long as it’s eating. “For that type of bird, it’s really driven by food,” Sheldon Harvey, vice president of Bird Protection Quebec, tells the Guardian. “As long as they can find food, their metabolism will keep them through the cold.”
The European robin’s appearance came just before the sighting of another unexpected bird—this one much bigger—on the other side of North America. On January 23, boat passengers off the coast of California caught sight of a waved albatross gliding above the water, far from its usual western Central and South American coastal range.

articles.title: These 12,000-Year-Old Scraps of Elk Hide May Be the World's Oldest Known Examples of Sewing
articles.author: Redação
articles.excerpt:
These 12,000-Year-Old Scraps of Elk Hide May Be the World’s Oldest Known Examples of Sewing
Indigenous groups in present-day Oregon stitched the fragments together using cord made from plant fiber...
articles.content:
These 12,000-Year-Old Scraps of Elk Hide May Be the World’s Oldest Known Examples of Sewing
Indigenous groups in present-day Oregon stitched the fragments together using cord made from plant fiber and animal hair. Experts think they may have been part of a garment, bag, container or portable shelter
Researchers say they may have identified the world’s oldest known evidence of sewing: two pieces of elk hide stitched together with cord. Found in a cave in Oregon, the tattered artifact was made around 12,000 years ago, during the end of the last Ice Age.
The fragment may have been “the margin of a piece of tight-fitted clothing, moccasin, bag or container, or part of a portable shelter,” though the researchers can’t “unequivocally confirm” its purpose, according to a new study in the journal Science Advances.
But despite these unknowns, the hide was “definitely sewn,” lead author Richard Rosencrance, an anthropologist at the University of Nevada, Reno, tells Science News’ Tom Metcalfe. “We have cordage sewn into a hide that comes right out and goes into another piece of hide.”
The researchers recently revisited troves of artifacts found in Oregon’s Cougar Mountain Cave and Paisley Caves. Per the study, these sites contained “two of the largest Late Pleistocene perishable assemblages in the world,” such as braided cords, bone needles, projectile points and wooden artifacts.
Through radiocarbon testing, the team determined the ages of 55 items, including the elk hide fragments, which had been found in Cougar Mountain Cave. They had been sewn together with cord made from plant fiber and animal hair between 12,600 and 11,880 years ago.
Indirect evidence of clothing production stretches back much earlier than 12,000 years, Rosencrance tells FOX 12 Now’s Greg Nibler. Researchers had previously discovered artifacts such as bone needles and figurines depicting figures wearing garments in Eurasia that are 50,000 years old. However, the Cougar Mountain Cave artifact is direct evidence of a sewn garment, making it particularly rare.
Experts have long known about the artifacts in Cougar Mountain Cave, which was excavated by amateur archaeologist John Cowles in 1958, according to the study. He kept the artifacts until his death in the 1980s, when they were transferred to the Favell Museum in Klamath Falls, Oregon. The trove became available for analysis only recently.
“This is kind of chronicling almost 12,000 years of shared technological knowledge,” Rosencrance says to Oregon Public Broadcasting’s Erik Neumann. As he tells FOX 12 Now, these artifacts demonstrate the close connection that Oregon’s Indigenous groups have to the land. “There’s few places in the world where you can look at a technological and cultural tradition and connect it over 10 or 12 millennia,” he says.
The Cougar Mountain and Paisley Caves are located in what’s now Oregon’s high desert. During the Younger Dryas, a period when temperatures dropped abruptly, the Indigenous groups who lived in the area used the caves as shelters. Their routines were driven by the seasons, Rosencrance explains to FOX 12 Now. They likely stocked up on food in the spring and summer, then spent autumns preparing for the harsh winters—perhaps by sewing warm clothes. By stitching materials together, these groups would have been able to make tight-fitting garments, which provided better insulation from the elements.
Quick fact: What was the Younger Dryas?
This period, which occurred between 12,900 and 11,600 years ago, was “characterized by cooler average temperatures that returned parts of Europe and North America to Ice Age conditions,” according to Encyclopedia Britannica.
“They were accomplished and serious sewists during the Ice Age,” Rosencrance tells Live Science’s Sophie Berdugo. “We already knew they [sewed clothes]; we just had to assume and guess what they were like.”
The cave artifacts include items made from a variety of plants, animals and types of wood. This resourcefulness shows that these groups had a “really deep, complex, traditional knowledge of the ecosystem around them,” which allowed them to “thrive in a really difficult time and place,” Rosencrance tells FOX 12 Now.
Artifacts of this kind are “extremely rare in the archaeological record,” according to the study. How, then, did the items in Oregon survive this long? The caves were an ideal shelter because they were dry—and over thousands of years, they kept the artifacts dry, too. As Rosencrance tells Oregon Public Broadcasting, “Being able to get a glimpse of what those things are really like and confirming what raw materials, what plants and animals, they used to make these things, is hardly ever attainable.”
